html:
H&R GmbH & Co. KGaA: ugly German refinery label, specialty cash-flow economics

H&R is being priced like a troubled German chemical refiner, but the current numbers point to something better: a family-controlled specialty hydrocarbon processor trading around 3x 2026 EBITDA, below 0.5x book equity, and around 4 to 6x normalized free cash flow if 2026 guidance is merely met.

The stale bull case was wrong on details. There is no obvious Walsrode ramp in the official 2025 report, capex did not collapse, and no 2025 dividend is being proposed. But the better thesis is still intact: 2025 was the cleanup year. H&R printed €83.8 million EBITDA, took heavy impairment charges, reported a shareholder loss, and still generated €35.4 million free cash flow. Q1 2026 then showed the forward economics: EBITDA was stable at €21.9 million, EBIT rose to €9.5 million, EPS doubled to €0.10, and free cash flow improved to €12.9 million versus negative €17.7 million a year earlier. Management maintained 2026 EBITDA guidance of €85 million to €100 million. That matters more than the reported 2025 loss.

This is not a fuel refiner. H&R’s two German refining sites in Hamburg and Salzbergen produce roughly 800 specialty products, including white oils, paraffins, plasticizers, base oils, lubricants, bitumen, feedstocks, and cable compounds. The company sells into cosmetics, pharma, food, construction, automotive, lubricants, and energy infrastructure. The official annual report describes fossil, renewable, synthetic, and recycled hydrocarbons as the feedstock base, which is exactly the type of messy transition story public markets often mislabel as structurally declining rather than cyclically and politically burdened.

The variant perception is simple. Screens see negative EPS, no dividend, German energy exposure, KGaA governance, a failed-looking tender offer, and a tiny illiquid share. What they miss is that the negative EPS was dominated by impairments, not cash burn. After those write-downs, depreciation normalized sharply in Q1 2026, so modest EBITDA stability now drops much harder into EBIT and EPS. At the May 18 Tradegate price of about €4.53, the equity value is only about €169 million. Using roughly €110 million of net financial debt, enterprise value is around €280 million. Against management’s €85 million to €100 million EBITDA guide, H&R trades around 2.8x to 3.3x EV/EBITDA.

The cash flow is the sharper point. Full-year 2025 free cash flow was €35 million despite €56 million of capex. Q1 2026 free cash flow was €13 million, helped by normalized working capital. I would not annualize Q1 blindly, but a €30 million to €45 million 2026 free cash flow range is realistic if EBITDA lands inside guidance and working capital does not re-inflate. That is an 18% to 27% FCF yield on the current equity. Even if capex remains structurally high because of German transformation spending, H&R does not need heroic growth to look cheap. It only needs to prove that 2025 impairments were a reset rather than the start of a capital sink.

The control situation is both risk and catalyst. H&R Holding offered €5.00 per share in 2025, increased its attributed stake to 77.54%, and said it may examine a delisting offer or squeeze-out if economically and operationally sensible. The stock now trades below that offer price. That is not a hard floor, but it changes the setup. The controlling family has already shown a willingness to buy more shares at a premium, and the free float has become even smaller. If operating results improve, the remaining minority becomes harder to buy cheaply. If results disappoint, the family can wait and public shareholders are trapped in an illiquid KGaA.

Retail context is useful here. German message-board discussion is thin and negative: wallstreet-online shows only light forum activity, with focus on Hansen control, a possibly low takeout price, raw-material risk, low liquidity, and speculative hydrogen or Power-to-X angles. That is not crowded small-cap enthusiasm. It is neglect plus distrust. The stock does not need a broad narrative to rerate. It needs a few quarters where EPS and FCF make the impairment-year optics look stale.

The bear case is real. H&R remains exposed to German energy costs, cheap non-EU or Russian-linked feedstocks, regulation, supply-chain shocks, and weak German industrial demand. The Plastics segment is still a drag, with Q1 EBITDA of negative €0.9 million. Management is also explicit that transformation investments may consume capital without near-term returns. This is why the stock deserves a discount to clean specialty chemical peers. But at 3x forward EBITDA, the discount already assumes a lot of permanent damage.

My base case is not a return to the old €20 share price. It is simpler: 2026 EBITDA around €92 million, forward EPS around €0.45 to €0.55, free cash flow around €35 million, and net financial debt trending down. A 4.5x EV/EBITDA multiple is still cheap for an asset-heavy specialty processor, but it implies roughly €8.50 per share. That is almost a double without assuming a dividend, a takeover, or peak-cycle spreads.

Bear, base, bull using current price of €4.53:

Bear: €3.30, down 27%. EBITDA falls toward €70 million, German cost pressure persists, Plastics keeps losing money, free cash flow is absorbed by capex, and the market applies a trapped-minority discount.

Base: €8.50, up 88%. EBITDA reaches the middle of the €85 million to €100 million guide, Q1 EPS quality continues, free cash flow lands around €35 million, and the stock rerates to 4.5x EBITDA.

Bull: €14.50, up 220%. EBITDA moves to €110 million to €115 million as specialty spreads tighten, refining utilization improves, Plastics stops bleeding, net debt falls, and the market or controlling family has to value H&R closer to 5.5x EBITDA.
